h1: India Nuclear Compliance After the SHANTI Act 2025 and the Bharat Small Reactor Program
meta_title: India Nuclear Compliance: SHANTI Act and BSR Guide
meta_description: India nuclear compliance after the SHANTI Act 2025 and Bharat Small Reactor program: licensing, liability caps, 49 percent FDI, AERB rules, vendor pathway.
tldr: India's nuclear compliance regime was rewritten on 20 December 2025 when the SHANTI Act received Presidential assent, repealing the Atomic Energy Act 1962 and the Civil Liability for Nuclear Damage Act 2010. The Act grants statutory status to the AERB, opens reactor build and operation to private companies with up to 49 percent foreign equity, removes automatic supplier liability, and sets graded operator caps from Rs 100 crore to Rs 3,000 crore. NPCIL leads the Bharat Small Reactor program of 220 MWe PHWRs for captive industrial use.
canonical_summary:
The SHANTI Act 2025, formally the Sustainable Harnessing and Advancement of Nuclear Energy for Transforming India Act, was passed by the Lok Sabha on 17 December 2025 and by the Rajya Sabha on 18 December 2025, with Presidential assent on 20 December 2025. It repealed the Atomic Energy Act 1962 and the Civil Liability for Nuclear Damage Act 2010 and consolidated India's nuclear governance into a single statute administered by the Department of Atomic Energy (DAE) with safety regulation by a now statutory Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB). Strategic activities including uranium and thorium mining, enrichment, reprocessing, heavy water production and off site spent fuel management remain reserved to the Central Government.

The Act introduces a graded operator liability framework that replaces the flat Rs 1,500 crore cap under the 2010 CLNDA, with limits ranging from approximately Rs 100 crore for the smallest reactors up to Rs 3,000 crore for plants above 500 MWe, calibrated to the 300 million SDR ceiling under the Convention on Supplementary Compensation for Nuclear Damage that India ratified in 2016. The statutory right of recourse against suppliers under Section 17(b) of the CLNDA has been removed; operators may only recover from suppliers through written contract terms or where intent to cause damage is established.

In parallel, NPCIL launched the Bharat Small Reactor (BSR) program through an RFP issued on 31 December 2024 for privately financed 220 MWe pressurised heavy water reactors aimed at captive industrial use. NPCIL retains design authority, operations and maintenance; the Rs 20,000 crore Nuclear Energy Mission announced in Union Budget 2025-26 funds parallel indigenous SMR development. Together with the NTPC and NPCIL joint venture ASHVINI and the March 2025 US Part 810 authorisation to Holtec International, these reforms reset the compliance baseline for every entity operating in or selling into the Indian nuclear market.

sections.heading: What changed on 20 December 2025: the SHANTI Act in force
paragraphs: The Sustainable Harnessing and Advancement of Nuclear Energy for Transforming India Act 2025 (the SHANTI Act) received Presidential assent on 20 December 2025 after passage in the Lok Sabha on 17 December and the Rajya Sabha on 18 December. The Act repeals two foundational statutes in a single instrument: the Atomic Energy Act 1962 and the Civil Liability for Nuclear Damage Act 2010. All licences, authorisations and approvals issued under those Acts are saved and read into the new framework, but every fresh licensing action from 2026 onward is governed by the SHANTI Act.

The Act consolidates four governance changes that compliance teams must internalise. First, the AERB is given statutory recognition with independent powers of licensing, inspection, suspension and penalty. Second, private companies and joint ventures may now apply for licences to build, own and operate reactors. Third, the liability regime moves from a single flat cap to a graded cap pegged to reactor capacity. Fourth, research and development activity is exempted from licensing where it does not involve criticality, in order to unlock private R&D investment in advanced reactors. The Department of Atomic Energy retains policy control and continues to administer fuel cycle activities reserved to the state.
bullets: Lok Sabha passage: 17 December 2025
Rajya Sabha passage: 18 December 2025
Presidential assent: 20 December 2025
Repealed: Atomic Energy Act 1962 and Civil Liability for Nuclear Damage Act 2010
AERB becomes a statutory regulator with inspection, suspension and penalty powers
Private and joint venture licensees admitted to reactor build and operation
Graded liability cap replaces the flat Rs 1,500 crore operator cap
R&D activity below criticality exempt from licensing

sections.heading: The Bharat Small Reactor program: structure, sites and the NPCIL operator model
paragraphs: The Bharat Small Reactor (BSR) program is NPCIL's vehicle for privately financed deployment of 220 MWe pressurised heavy water reactors derived from the IPHWR-220 design. NPCIL floated the Request for Proposal on 31 December 2024 and extended the submission deadline to 31 March 2026 in response to industry queries. The commercial structure separates capital from operations: the private partner provides land, cooling water, site preparation and full lifecycle finance including decommissioning, while NPCIL retains design authority, owns the plant on completion, and operates and maintains it under AERB licence. Electricity is delivered for captive use by the financing industrial user; surplus may be sold at a DAE determined tariff.

Six industrial groups have submitted expressions of interest under the RFP: Reliance Industries, Adani Power, Tata Power, JSW Energy, Jindal Steel and Power, and Hindalco Industries. The target customer base is energy intensive manufacturing including steel, aluminium, copper smelting and cement. Parallel to the BSR, the Department of Atomic Energy is developing a separate 200 MWe Bharat Small Modular Reactor (BSMR) design through BARC, with first units planned at Tarapur in Maharashtra and at the BARC Visakhapatnam campus in Andhra Pradesh. The Nuclear Energy Mission announced in Union Budget 2025-26 carries an outlay of Rs 20,000 crore and commits the government to operationalising at least five indigenously developed SMRs by 2033.
bullets: Reactor: 220 MWe pressurised heavy water reactor based on IPHWR-220
RFP issued 31 December 2024; submission deadline 31 March 2026
Private partner finances build, land and decommissioning; NPCIL designs, owns and operates
Bidders confirmed by NPCIL include Reliance, Adani, Tata Power, JSW, Jindal Steel, Hindalco
Indigenous BSMR (200 MWe) planned for Tarapur and Visakhapatnam
Nuclear Energy Mission outlay Rs 20,000 crore in Union Budget 2025-26
Target: at least 5 SMRs operational by 2033, 40 to 50 BSR units in the next decade
National goal: 100 GWe of nuclear capacity by 2047

sections.heading: AERB licensing pathway for SMRs and large reactors under the SHANTI Act
paragraphs: The AERB licensing pathway under the SHANTI Act follows the consent stages familiar from the earlier AERB Safety Code on Regulation of Nuclear and Radiation Facilities (AERB/SC/G), now exercised under statutory authority. Applicants progress through siting consent, construction consent, commissioning authorisation including a first approach to criticality clearance, operating licence, and eventually decommissioning authorisation. The Act also requires periodic safety review at intervals set by the AERB and gives the regulator the power to suspend, vary or cancel any consent. For SMRs the AERB has confirmed that its existing code framework will be supplemented with SMR specific provisions addressing modular construction, passive safety systems, multi unit siting and factory fabrication quality assurance.

Private operators must satisfy two parallel approvals. The first is the policy licence issued by the Central Government under the SHANTI Act, which establishes eligibility to construct and operate a nuclear power plant. The second is the AERB consent chain that authorises actual construction, fuel load, commercial operation and continued operation. Operators retain primary responsibility for safety. Suppliers and engineering, procurement and construction partners must comply with AERB codes on design, manufacture, transport and installation, and are subject to inspection both at vendor premises and on site.
bullets: Consent stages: siting, construction, commissioning, operating licence, decommissioning
Dual approval required: SHANTI Act licence from the Central Government plus AERB consents
AERB safety codes (AERB/SC/G and reactor specific codes) continue to apply under statutory authority
SMR specific provisions under development for modular construction and passive safety
Periodic safety review and renewal at AERB defined intervals
Vendor inspection rights extend to manufacturing premises outside India

sections.heading: Civil liability regime: graded operator caps and the removal of statutory supplier recourse
paragraphs: The SHANTI Act replaces the flat Rs 1,500 crore operator liability cap that the CLNDA 2010 applied to reactors of 10 MW thermal and above with a graded cap that scales with reactor capacity. Public reporting on the Act describes operator liability bands from approximately Rs 100 crore at the lower end up to Rs 3,000 crore for plants above 500 MWe, capped overall at 300 million Special Drawing Rights consistent with India's obligations under the Convention on Supplementary Compensation for Nuclear Damage (CSC), which India ratified on 4 February 2016. The Act creates a Nuclear Liability Fund as the domestic top up layer and confirms access to the CSC international fund for transboundary incidents.

The most consequential change for vendors is the treatment of the operator's right of recourse against suppliers. Section 17(b) of the CLNDA 2010, together with Section 46, created a statutory right for operators to recover from suppliers where an incident arose from a patent or latent defect or sub standard services. Under the SHANTI Act this statutory route is removed. Operators may now recover from suppliers only on terms expressly set out in the written contract between operator and supplier, or where the supplier or an individual acted with intent to cause nuclear damage. Insurance availability for both operators and suppliers should improve in line with this realignment with international practice, and the Indian Nuclear Insurance Pool will be repositioned to underwrite the new caps.
bullets: Graded operator liability cap approximately Rs 100 crore to Rs 3,000 crore by capacity
Overall ceiling aligned to 300 million SDR under the CSC
Statutory supplier recourse under Section 17(b) and Section 46 of the CLNDA 2010 removed
Operator recourse against suppliers only via written contract or proof of intent
Nuclear Liability Fund established as domestic top up layer
CSC access available for transboundary or major incidents
Indian Nuclear Insurance Pool to be re scoped to the new caps

sections.heading: Foreign vendor pathway: 49 percent FDI cap, Part 810 and reserved activities
paragraphs: Foreign participation under the SHANTI Act flows through two parallel channels. On the equity side, the Government has confirmed that nuclear power generation will accept foreign direct investment of up to 49 percent through joint ventures with an Indian operator. Indian partners retain majority equity and the Department of Atomic Energy must approve the structure. Sovereign wealth funds and strategic equity partners can therefore participate as financiers and not merely as engineering, procurement and construction vendors. On the technology side, US origin technology continues to require US Department of Energy authorisation under 10 CFR Part 810. The first specific authorisation for an SMR in India was issued to Holtec International on 26 March 2025 for the SMR-300 design, naming Larsen and Toubro, Tata Consulting Engineers and Holtec Asia as eligible counterparties.

A set of activities remains reserved to the Central Government and is not open to private or foreign participation. These include uranium and thorium mining, isotopic separation and enrichment, reprocessing of spent fuel, production of heavy water, and management of high level waste and off site spent fuel beyond on site storage. Foreign vendors targeting India should map every component of their commercial proposition against this reserved list and confirm that their offering sits inside the private domain. Vendors based in jurisdictions other than the United States should verify analogous export control authorisations in their home country and confirm that any technology transferred is consistent with safeguards obligations administered under India's IAEA Additional Protocol.
bullets: Up to 49 percent foreign direct investment permitted in nuclear power joint ventures
Indian partner retains majority equity and DAE approval is required
US technology transfers continue to require 10 CFR Part 810 authorisation
Holtec SMR-300 Part 810 authorisation granted 26 March 2025 (Authorisation IN-2023-001)
Eligible Indian counterparties named: Larsen and Toubro, Tata Consulting Engineers, Holtec Asia
Reserved to the Central Government: mining, enrichment, reprocessing, heavy water, high level waste
Foreign vendors must verify home country export control parity

sections.heading: Timeline and active projects: ASHVINI, Kudankulam, Mahi Banswara and BSR
paragraphs: The institutional anchor of the new build pipeline is ASHVINI, formally Anushakti Vidhyut Nigam Limited, a joint venture of NPCIL (51 percent) and NTPC Limited (49 percent) approved by the Government on 11 September 2024 and restructured on 9 January 2025 with authorised share capital expanded from Rs 5 crore to Rs 15,000 crore. The Mahi Banswara Rajasthan Atomic Power Project, four units of 700 MWe based on the indigenous PHWR design, was transferred from NPCIL to ASHVINI. ASHVINI is positioned to take up additional sites and is expected to be one of the first vehicles to test the SHANTI Act licensing framework for a major new build.

Existing fleet build continues in parallel. Kudankulam Unit 5 reached the reactor pressure vessel installation milestone in June 2026 under the India and Russia framework. The construction pipeline including units at Kakrapar, Rajasthan and Gorakhpur Haryana brings installed capacity from approximately 8.2 GWe today toward the interim 22.48 GWe target by 2031-32. The BSR submission window closes on 31 March 2026, after which NPCIL is expected to enter site appraisal and contract negotiation with shortlisted industrial partners. First BSR concrete pour is expected in the second half of the decade with a 60 to 72 week per unit construction profile guiding the cumulative target of 40 to 50 BSR units within ten years.
bullets: ASHVINI: NPCIL 51 percent and NTPC 49 percent joint venture
ASHVINI authorised share capital raised to Rs 15,000 crore on 9 January 2025
Mahi Banswara 4 x 700 MWe PHWR transferred from NPCIL to ASHVINI
Kudankulam Unit 5 reactor pressure vessel installed June 2026
Installed nuclear capacity approximately 8.2 GWe today, 22.48 GWe targeted by 2031-32
BSR submission deadline 31 March 2026
100 GWe national target by 2047

sections.heading: US India nuclear cooperation after SHANTI: 123 Agreement, Part 810 and entity list changes
paragraphs: The SHANTI Act has reactivated the US India civil nuclear track that was set in legislative form by the 2008 India and United States Agreement for Cooperation Concerning Peaceful Uses of Nuclear Energy, commonly called the 123 Agreement. Until 2025 the operating constraint was a combination of the supplier liability regime under Section 17(b) of the CLNDA, the absence of statutory standing for the AERB, and US export control restrictions on three Indian nuclear entities. On 16 January 2025 the US Department of Commerce removed the Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, the Indira Gandhi Centre for Atomic Research and Indian Rare Earths from the Entity List. The Department of Energy followed with the Part 810 specific authorisation to Holtec on 26 March 2025.

The SHANTI Act closes the third constraint by aligning India's liability regime with the Convention on Supplementary Compensation and removing automatic supplier liability. The result is a workable commercial pathway for US reactor vendors covering large light water reactors, small modular reactors and advanced designs to enter India through joint ventures with Indian operators and licensed Indian partners. US officials have publicly welcomed the SHANTI Act and indicated readiness for joint research and development cooperation. Compliance teams should not, however, treat the entity list and Part 810 changes as a single approval: each transaction continues to require its own Part 810 review or general authorisation under 10 CFR 810.6, and certain end uses still require specific authorisation and Indian Government concurrence.
bullets: 123 Agreement remains the underlying framework for US India civil nuclear cooperation
BARC, IGCAR and Indian Rare Earths removed from US Entity List on 16 January 2025
Holtec SMR-300 specific authorisation issued 26 March 2025 under 10 CFR 810.9
Each transaction requires its own Part 810 general or specific authorisation
US Government has welcomed SHANTI and signalled openness to joint R&D
Indian Government concurrence remains a precondition for specific authorisations

sections.heading: What compliance teams should do now
paragraphs: Compliance leads at Indian SMR developers, ASHVINI partner organisations and foreign vendors should treat the period from January 2026 onward as the regulatory transition window during which AERB will publish subordinate rules, codes and SMR specific provisions under the SHANTI Act. Three immediate work streams matter. The first is contractual: every operator and supplier contract should be reviewed against the new Clause 16 recourse regime to confirm that recourse rights are captured in writing where commercially intended, that intent based liability is allocated, and that insurance covers the new graded operator cap. The second is licensing: existing AERB consents and pending applications should be mapped onto the new licence categories under the SHANTI Act, and gaps in safety documentation flagged before the AERB issues fresh procedural rules.

The third stream is governance and disclosure. Joint venture structures involving foreign equity must demonstrate that the 49 percent FDI ceiling and the reserved activities list are honoured at every layer of the corporate structure. Boards of private operators and vendors should adopt nuclear compliance policies covering AERB inspection cooperation, safeguards reporting under the IAEA Additional Protocol, and US Part 810 record keeping where applicable. Invariant works with Indian and foreign teams to build the underlying control framework: licence tracking, supplier liability mapping, Part 810 evidence files and AERB inspection readiness. The window between SHANTI Act assent and the first private BSR contract award is the right time to put this framework in place.
bullets: Review all operator and supplier contracts against Clause 16 of the SHANTI Act
Map existing AERB consents to the new statutory licence categories
Confirm insurance cover under the new graded operator cap
Audit JV cap table for the 49 percent FDI ceiling at every layer
Verify no commercial activity touches the reserved fuel cycle list
Stand up Part 810 record keeping for any US origin technology
Track AERB rule making for SMR specific provisions throughout 2026
Document Additional Protocol reporting and safeguards interfaces

faqs.question: When did the SHANTI Act come into force in India?
faqs.answer: The Sustainable Harnessing and Advancement of Nuclear Energy for Transforming India Act 2025 was passed by the Lok Sabha on 17 December 2025 and by the Rajya Sabha on 18 December 2025. President Droupadi Murmu granted assent on 20 December 2025. The Act repealed the Atomic Energy Act 1962 and the Civil Liability for Nuclear Damage Act 2010. Licences and authorisations issued under the repealed statutes are saved into the new framework, but all fresh licensing actions from 2026 are governed by the SHANTI Act and the subordinate rules to be issued by the Atomic Energy Regulatory Board under its new statutory powers.

faqs.question: Can private Indian companies own and operate nuclear reactors after the SHANTI Act?
faqs.answer: Yes. The SHANTI Act removes the state monopoly that the Atomic Energy Act 1962 had placed on reactor build and operation. Private companies registered under the Companies Act 2013 may now apply for licences to construct, own and operate nuclear power plants, either directly or through joint ventures. The actual licence is issued by the Central Government, and AERB consents are required at the siting, construction, commissioning, operation and decommissioning stages. Sensitive fuel cycle activities including mining, enrichment, reprocessing, heavy water production and off site spent fuel management remain reserved to the Central Government.

faqs.question: What is the foreign direct investment ceiling for nuclear power in India?
faqs.answer: Foreign direct investment is permitted up to 49 percent in nuclear power generation joint ventures, with the Indian partner retaining majority equity and the Department of Atomic Energy approving the structure. This is the first time foreign capital has been allowed direct equity exposure to Indian nuclear power generation. The ceiling applies at every layer of the corporate structure, so foreign investors should confirm that downstream holdings, special purpose vehicles and parent guarantees do not breach the cap in aggregate. Activities reserved to the Central Government remain closed to foreign participation entirely.

faqs.question: What is the Bharat Small Reactor program and how is it different from the BSMR?
faqs.answer: The Bharat Small Reactor (BSR) is NPCIL's 220 MWe pressurised heavy water reactor, derived from the IPHWR-220 design, to be deployed under a captive use commercial model where a private industrial partner finances the project and NPCIL operates the plant. NPCIL issued the RFP on 31 December 2024 with a 31 March 2026 submission deadline. The Bharat Small Modular Reactor (BSMR) is a parallel 200 MWe indigenous SMR design developed through BARC under the Rs 20,000 crore Nuclear Energy Mission, with first units planned at Tarapur and the BARC Visakhapatnam campus. The two programs run in parallel.

faqs.question: Which Indian companies have responded to the BSR RFP?
faqs.answer: NPCIL has publicly identified six industrial groups as participants in the BSR Request for Proposal process: Reliance Industries, Adani Power, Tata Power, JSW Energy, Jindal Steel and Power, and Hindalco Industries. The targeted end use industries are energy intensive manufacturing including steel, aluminium, copper smelting and cement, where the captive 220 MWe reactor model is intended to displace fossil fuel based captive generation. The submission deadline was extended from 30 September 2025 to 31 March 2026 in response to industry queries and is the current cutoff.

faqs.question: Does the SHANTI Act remove supplier liability under the CLNDA?
faqs.answer: The SHANTI Act removes the statutory right of recourse that Section 17(b) of the CLNDA 2010 gave operators against suppliers for patent or latent defects, and the parallel preservation of civil and criminal action under Section 46 of the CLNDA. Under the SHANTI Act, operators may now pursue suppliers only on terms expressly set out in the written contract between the parties, or where the supplier or an individual acted with intent to cause nuclear damage. This brings India in line with the channelling principle of the Convention on Supplementary Compensation that India ratified in February 2016.

faqs.question: What are the new operator liability caps?
faqs.answer: The SHANTI Act replaces the flat Rs 1,500 crore operator liability cap of the CLNDA 2010 with a graded cap calibrated to reactor capacity. Public reporting and the legislative summary describe limits ranging from approximately Rs 100 crore for the smallest reactors to Rs 3,000 crore for plants above 500 MWe, with an overall ceiling aligned to 300 million Special Drawing Rights under the Convention on Supplementary Compensation. A domestic Nuclear Liability Fund is established as a top up layer and access to the CSC international fund is available for transboundary or major incidents.

faqs.question: What is ASHVINI and what role does it play?
faqs.answer: ASHVINI, formally Anushakti Vidhyut Nigam Limited, is a joint venture of Nuclear Power Corporation of India Limited (51 percent) and NTPC Limited (49 percent) approved by the Government on 11 September 2024. On 9 January 2025 the parties signed a supplementary joint venture agreement that raised the authorised share capital from Rs 5 crore to Rs 15,000 crore. The Mahi Banswara Rajasthan Atomic Power Project, four units of 700 MWe based on the indigenous PHWR design, was transferred from NPCIL to ASHVINI. ASHVINI is expected to bid for additional sites and to be among the earliest vehicles to operate under the new SHANTI Act licensing framework.

faqs.question: How does the AERB licensing pathway work for a new private SMR build?
faqs.answer: A private SMR developer needs two parallel approvals. The first is the policy licence issued by the Central Government under the SHANTI Act, confirming eligibility to construct and operate a nuclear power plant. The second is the AERB consent chain at the siting, construction, commissioning including first approach to criticality, operating licence and decommissioning stages. AERB safety codes continue to apply under statutory authority. AERB has confirmed that SMR specific provisions will be added covering modular construction, passive safety systems, multi unit siting and factory fabrication quality assurance. Periodic safety review is required at AERB defined intervals.

faqs.question: What is the US 10 CFR Part 810 authorisation for Holtec and why does it matter?
faqs.answer: On 26 March 2025 the US Department of Energy issued specific authorisation IN-2023-001 to Holtec International under 10 CFR 810.9 to transfer unclassified small modular reactor technology, specifically the SMR-300 design, to named Indian counterparties Larsen and Toubro, Tata Consulting Engineers and Holtec Asia. The authorisation runs for ten years with a five year review and is limited to peaceful use under IAEA safeguards. It matters because it is the first SMR specific Part 810 authorisation for India and signals the operating template under which other US vendors can pursue equivalent authorisations to share technology with Indian counterparties.

faqs.question: Are BARC, IGCAR and Indian Rare Earths still on the US Entity List?
faqs.answer: No. On 16 January 2025 the US Department of Commerce removed Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Indira Gandhi Centre for Atomic Research and Indian Rare Earths from the Entity List. The delisting was an explicit step toward operationalising the India and United States Agreement for Cooperation Concerning Peaceful Uses of Nuclear Energy, the 2008 123 Agreement. Vendors and integrators should still confirm transaction specific export control posture under the Export Administration Regulations and, for US origin technology, under 10 CFR Part 810 for the Department of Energy and 10 CFR Part 110 for the Nuclear Regulatory Commission as applicable.

faqs.question: Did the United States Congress pass its own SHANTI Act?
faqs.answer: No. The SHANTI Act is Indian legislation. The United States Government has publicly welcomed the SHANTI Act and indicated readiness to deepen civil nuclear cooperation with India and to pursue joint research and development. Operational US measures alongside the Indian Act include the January 2025 Entity List delisting of BARC, IGCAR and Indian Rare Earths and the March 2025 Part 810 specific authorisation to Holtec International. There is no separate US Congressional bill of the same name; US executive branch action under existing authorities including the 2008 123 Agreement is the operating vehicle.

faqs.question: How does the SHANTI Act interact with India's IAEA safeguards obligations?
faqs.answer: India remains a party to its India specific safeguards agreement with the IAEA and the corresponding Additional Protocol that entered into force in 2014. Civilian reactors notified by India to the IAEA continue to be under safeguards. The SHANTI Act does not alter this regime. Private operators and foreign vendors should expect that any civilian reactor licensed under the SHANTI Act will be a safeguarded facility, that material accountancy and reporting obligations flow through the operator to the Department of Atomic Energy and the IAEA, and that supplier transfers of US, French, Russian or Japanese origin technology will continue to carry their own safeguards conditions through bilateral agreements.

faqs.question: What practical steps should an Indian SMR developer take in the first half of 2026?
faqs.answer: First, build a single source of truth for licences, AERB consents and pending approvals, mapped against the SHANTI Act licence categories. Second, audit operator and supplier contracts against the new Clause 16 recourse regime so that intended recourse rights are reduced to writing and intent based liability is allocated. Third, confirm insurance cover under the new graded operator cap. Fourth, if a foreign equity partner is involved, audit the cap table at every layer for the 49 percent ceiling. Fifth, if US origin technology is involved, stand up Part 810 record keeping. Sixth, track AERB rule making for SMR specific provisions through 2026.

faqs.question: Is the BSR captive use model exempt from grid connection rules?
faqs.answer: No. The BSR commercial model is captive use by an industrial partner that finances the plant, but the reactor is licensed and operated by NPCIL under the SHANTI Act and AERB consents, and surplus electricity may be sold to other customers at a Department of Atomic Energy determined tariff under existing electricity sector regulations. Grid connection, scheduling and ancillary services obligations under the Indian Electricity Grid Code and the Central Electricity Regulatory Commission framework continue to apply where the plant is interconnected. Captive status under the Electricity Act 2003 must be separately demonstrated for the industrial partner.
